elements.sent-2411-001: Background Laboratory values are fundamental to medical diagnosis and management, but acquiring these values can be costly, invasive, and time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-002: While electrocardiogram (ECG) patterns have been linked to certain laboratory abnormalities, the comprehensive modeling of these relationships remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-003: Methods We utilize MIMIC-IV dataset to develop multimodal deep-learning models to demonstrate the feasibility of estimating (real-time) and monitoring (predict at future intervals) laboratory value abnormalities from ECG waveforms, demographics, biometrics, and vital signs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Results The models exhibit a strong predictive performance with AUROC scores above 0.70 in a statistically significant manner for 23 laboratory values in the estimation setting and up to 26 values in the monitoring setting.
elements.sent-2411-005: Most notably, the accurately predictable values encompassing abnormalities across diverse physiological categories such as cardiac, renal, hematological, metabolic, immunological and coagulation.
elements.sent-2411-006: To name examples, for estimation NTproBNP ($\geq$353 pg/mL) with 0.882, whereas for monitoring at 30 minutes Urea nitrogen ($\leq$6 mg/dL) with 0.851, at 60 minutes creatinine ($\leq$0.5 mg/dL) with 0.85, and at 120 minutes hemoglobin ($\geq$17.5 g/dL) with 0.821.
elements.sent-2411-007: Conclusions This study provides first evidence for the feasibility of using ECG data alongside clinical routine data for the real-time estimation and monitoring of laboratory value abnormalities, which could provide a non-invasive, cost-effective supplement to traditional laboratory testing, with strong implications for enhanced patient monitoring and early intervention.
elements.sent-2411-008: Further validation could facilitate their integration into routine clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-009: Laboratory values play a crucial role in various medical contexts, ranging from diagnosing diseases \cite{chernecky2012laboratory to guiding therapeutic interventions in routine care \cite{rigby2017review and intensive care \cite{ezzie2007laboratory settings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These values, derived from various bodily fluids or tissues, provide quantitative insights into a patients' physiological status, aiding clinicians in making informed decisions.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite their importance, obtaining laboratory values often involves invasive procedures, such as venipuncture or arterial puncture, which can cause discomfort and carry associated risks \cite{ialongo2016phlebotomy,buowari2013complications .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, the process is resource-intensive \cite{beliveau2018decreasing,halpern2004critical , requiring specialized equipment, skilled personnel, and significant time for sample collection, processing, and analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Delays in obtaining results further limit their utility in rapidly evolving clinical scenarios, highlighting the need for alternative or complementary approaches of measurement \cite{vashist2012non,shah2014accuracy .}
elements.sent-2411-014: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is essential as a first-in-line assessment of a patient's cardiac state.
elements.sent-2411-015: While the ECG is traditionally associated with the detection of cardiovascular diseases, the advent of deep learning has expanded the diagnostic potential of the ECG towards other domains, such as non-cardiac conditions, reviewed in \cite{topol2021s,siontis2021artificial and demonstrated within a single model in \cite{strodthoff2024prospects , or as in important predictor for patient deterioration \cite{alcaraz2024mdsedmultimodaldecisionsupport .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recent research has explored the relationship between cardiac patterns observed on ECG and abnormal laboratory values, suggesting potential links between systemic health and cardiovascular function, and while advancements in machine learning have enabled ECG to model diverse physiological factors beyond cardiovascular health, the integration of ECG with machine learning towards modeling laboratory values presents new opportunities for learning complex interactions which can enhance clinical decision making \cite{hager2024evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Finally, \cite{tan2021atrial demonstrates that abnormal ECG patterns, interact with biomarkers to influence heart failure risk predictions such as NT-proBNP.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This work stands in the tradition of an earlier conference publication \cite{alcaraz2024cardiolablaboratoryvaluesestimation , where the authors demonstrated the feasibility of predicting anomalous laboratory values from ECG features and demographics.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This work significantly extends this work by replacing ECG features by ECG waveforms leveraging deep learning models and extending the application scenario from estimation to estimation and monitoring.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{abstract.pdf} \caption{Schematic overview of the study. A) Illustration of the workflow used in the study, where for model inputs we use ECG waveforms, demographics, biometrics, and vital signs, in a binary classification setting are used to predict abnormal laboratory values. B) Demonstration of the estimation task, where we sample the closest vital signs within 30 minutes around the ECG record and use the closest laboratory value within 60 minutes as prediction target. C) Demonstration of the monitoring task, where we also use closest vital signs within 30 minutes around the ECG record as input but where we predict the presence of any abnormal laboratory value within a predefined time horizon.} \label{fig:abstract}
elements.sent-2411-020: The dataset for this study integrates the publicly available MIMIC-IV \cite{johnson2020mimic and MIMIC-IV-ECG \cite{MIMICIVECG2023 databases, comprising 795,546 samples from 159,608 unique patients, including both inpatient and outpatient records.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Each sample features a 12-lead ECG recorded over 10 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-022: Demographic data includes gender, age, and race (Caucasian, African, Asian, Latino, or other).
elements.sent-2411-023: Vital signs, which are practical and cost-effective for non-invasive measurement, include temperature (°F), heart rate (bpm), respiration rate (bpm), oxygen saturation (\%), systolic and diastolic blood pressure (mmHg).
elements.sent-2411-024: Biometrics such as BMI (kg/m²), weight (kg), and height (cm) are also included.
elements.sent-2411-025: These features support binary classification tasks to predict abnormalities in laboratory values, defined by median lab-value-specific thresholds (distributed with MIMIC-IV) across the dataset (see Figure~\ref{fig:abstract A).}
elements.sent-2411-026: The predictive tasks include estimation and monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-027: The estimation task, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:abstract B, utilizes vital signs from 30 minutes collected before or after the ECG was taken to predict laboratory abnormalities based on the closest value within 60 minutes.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The monitoring task, depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:abstract C, uses the same vital signs to forecast if any abnormality within 30, 60, or 120 minutes in the future.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Data splitting follows the MIMIC-IV-ECG-ICD \cite{strodthoff2024prospects approach, with an 18:1:1 train-validation-test ratio, stratified according to diagnoses.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We require at least 10 positive and 10 negative cases per fold, resulting in 137 abnormalities for estimation, and 116, 126, and 135 abnormalities for monitoring at time horizons 30, 60, and 120 minutes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-031: The primary model used is an S4 classifier, consisting of a time series encoder with four bidirectional S4 blocks \cite{Gu2021EfficientlyML , each with a dimension of 512 and a state size of 8, followed by average pooling as used in prior work\cite{mehari2023towards,strodthoff2024prospects,alcaraz2024mdsedmultimodaldecisionsupport .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The tabular encoder consist of a linear layer with PReLu activation function aiming to align the size of tabular features with the time series latent representations.
elements.sent-2411-033: Then, we concatenate both latent representations and employ a late fusion strategy with a differentiable outer product \cite{zadeh2017tensor and a linear classification head.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Missing values in tabular features are median-imputed.
elements.sent-2411-035: We use additional binary variables to track missing input variables in the feature space.
elements.sent-2411-036: We considered all abnormalities with at least 10 positive and 10 negative labels in the validation and test folds respectively.
elements.sent-2411-037: We optimize a binary crossentropy loss as appropriate for a multi-label classification problem.
elements.sent-2411-038: We exclude contributions from samples for which no target value is available from the computation of the loss, separately for each laboratory value.
elements.sent-2411-039: Models are optimized with the AdamW optimizer using a learning rate and weight decay of 0.001, and trained for 20 epochs with a batch size of 32.
elements.sent-2411-040: To avoid overfitting, we select models based on the highest macro AUROC on the validation set, typically around epoch 15.
elements.sent-2411-041: Following research by \cite{mehari2023towards , we use 2.5-second ECG segments.}
elements.sent-2411-042: During testing, predictions are averaged over four non-overlapping 2.5-second segments sampled at 100 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-043: Training takes about 19 hours on an NVIDIA L40 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-044: Statistical uncertainty is assessed using empirical bootstrap with 1000 iterations, providing 95\% prediction intervals for macro AUROC and individual AUROCs.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Performance results for laboratory values estimation with AUROC exceeding 0.7 (i.e., lower bound of the AUROC score larger than 0.7), presented by value name, threshold and nature of the abnormality, and corresponding AUROC scores with 95\% prediction intervals. Next to each value name, we highlight the physiological category it represents: Cardiac (Ca.), Renal (Re.), Hematological (He.), Metabolic (Me.), Immunological (Im.), and Coagulation (Co.). } \begin{tabular}{lccc} Value Threshold Estimation AUROC NTproBNP [Ca.] $\geq$353 pg/mL 0.882 (0.860, 0.902) Urea Nitrogen [Re.] $\leq$6 mg/dL 0.860 (0.822, 0.890) Hemoglobin [He.] $\geq$17.5 g/dL 0.844 (0.779, 0.900) Creatinine [Re./Ca.] $\leq$0.5 mg/dL 0.825 (0.792, 0.857) Ferritin [Me.] $\leq$13 ng/mL 0.820 (0.716, 0.910) Creatinine [Re./Ca.] $\geq$1.2 mg/dL 0.797 (0.789, 0.806) Hematocrit [He.] $\geq$51 \% 0.789 (0.731, 0.840) Albumin [Me.] $\leq$3.5 g/dL 0.786 (0.767, 0.805) Urea Nitrogen [Re.] $\geq$20 mg/dL 0.781 (0.773, 0.788) RDW-SD [He.] $\geq$46.3 fL 0.769 (0.753, 0.784) Hemoglobin [He.] $\leq$13.7 g/dL 0.762 (0.752, 0.771) Anion Gap [Me.] $\geq$20 mEq/L 0.745 (0.728, 0.761) RDW [He.] $\geq$15.5 \% 0.742 (0.733, 0.753) Bicarbonate [Me.] $\geq$32 mEq/L 0.739 (0.712, 0.766) Bilirubin, Total [Me.] $\geq$1.5 mg/dL 0.734 (0.707, 0.761) Hematocrit [He.] $\leq$40 \% 0.734 (0.725, 0.744) INR PT [Co.] $\geq$1.1 ratio 0.734 (0.723, 0.746) Red Blood Cells [He.] $\leq$4.6 m/uL 0.732 (0.722, 0.742) PT [Co.] $\geq$12.5 sec 0.731 (0.720, 0.743) Creatine Kinase [Ca.] $\leq$38 IU/L 0.730 (0.703, 0.758) MCHC [He.] $\leq$31 g/dL 0.726 (0.710, 0.741) Calcium, Total [Re.] $\leq$8.4 mg/dL 0.722 (0.705, 0.738) Lymphocytes [Im.] $\leq$18 \% 0.720 (0.710, 0.731) \end{tabular} \label{table:est}
elements.sent-2411-045: Table \ref{table:est contains the performance results for laboratory value estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Overall, 23 abnormalities across 19 unique laboratory values achieve AUROC scores above 0.7 in a statistically significant manner.
elements.sent-2411-047: Notably, NTproBNP levels above 353 pg/mL were estimated with an AUROC of 0.882.
elements.sent-2411-048: Urea nitrogen levels below 6 mg/dL were estimated with an AUROC of 0.860.
elements.sent-2411-049: Hemoglobin levels above 17.5 g/dL had an AUROC of 0.844, while Creatinine levels below 0.5 mg/dL with an AUROC of 0.825.
elements.sent-2411-050: Other values such as ferritin, hematocrit, and albumin also demonstrated strong predictive performance.
elements.sent-2411-051: Table \ref{table:mon contains the performance results for laboratory value monitoring.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Overall, the number of abnormal conditions and unique laboratory values that achieve an AUROC score above 0.7 in a statistically significant manner are 15 and 13 for the 30-minute horizon, 25 and 22 for the 60-minutes horizon, as well as 26 and 22 for the 120-minute horizon.
elements.sent-2411-053: Notably, NTproBNP levels above 353 pg/mL achieved the highest AUROC of 0.881 at 120 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-054: Urea nitrogen levels below 6 mg/dL had an AUROC of 0.854 at 120 minutes, while urea nitrogen levels above 20 mg/dL reached an AUROC of 0.787 at 120 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-055: Creatinine levels below 0.5 mg/dL performed best with an AUROC of 0.833 at 60 minutes, and creatinine levels above 1.2 mg/dL had an AUROC of 0.804 at 120 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Hemoglobin levels above 17.5 g/dL showed a significant AUROC of 0.821 at 120 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-057: Iron binding and transferrin levels below 260 ug/dL and 200 mg/dL, respectively, both achieved an AUROC of 0.803 at 60 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-058: Hematocrit levels above 51\% had an AUROC of 0.797 at 120 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-059: Finally, albumin levels below 3.5 g/dL achieved an AUROC of 0.786 at 60 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-060: Other values such as RDW-SD, eAG, absolute basophils, bicarbonate, anion gap, RDW, creatine kinase, monocytes, MCHC, bilirubin, red blood cells, lymphocytes, INR PT, calcium, and PT also demonstrated strong predictive performance.
elements.sent-2411-061: Utilizing the MIMIC-IV dataset, multimodal deep-learning models were developed to predict abnormalities in real time and at future intervals.
elements.sent-2411-062: The models achieved strong performance, with AUROC scores above 0.70 for up to 26 laboratory values across various physiological categories.
elements.sent-2411-063: Key results include accurate predictions for NTproBNP, urea nitrogen, creatinine, and hemoglobin levels.
elements.sent-2411-064: These findings demonstrate the feasibility of non-invasive, cost-effective methods for patient monitoring, enhancing the way for diagnostic tools and early interventions in clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-065: Cardiac markers such as NTproBNP, creatinine, and creatine kinase are directly related to heart stress and injury, with ECG changes providing insight into these conditions.
elements.sent-2411-066: Renal markers like urea nitrogen, creatinine, and calcium can cause cardiac function disturbances due to fluid and electrolyte imbalances, making ECG prediction plausible \cite{boudoulas2017cardio .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Hematological values, including hemoglobin, hematocrit, and RDW, are linked to blood disorders affecting cardiac function, which can be mirrored in ECG changes.
elements.sent-2411-068: Metabolic markers such as bicarbonate and anion gap influence cardiac function through disturbances like acidosis or alkalosis, detectable by ECG.
elements.sent-2411-069: Immunological parameters like iron binding and lymphocytes may indirectly affect cardiac health through systemic inflammation, which can be reflected in ECG patterns \cite{swirski2018cardioimmunology .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Finally, coagulation markers like INR PT and PT are essential for assessing bleeding and clotting risks, with their abnormalities potentially causing ECG changes due to associated cardiac stress.
elements.sent-2411-071: The monitoring aligns with prior work highlighting the added diagnostic value of ECG waveforms for deterioration task \cite{alcaraz2024mdsedmultimodaldecisionsupport .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Nevertheless, the prediction of anomalous lab values represents a prediction task with a well-defined ground truth which makes it a relevant case for algorithmic benchmarking.
elements.sent-2411-073: Clinical implications of predicting laboratory values from ECG data, such as potential benefits in patient monitoring, early diagnosis, treatment personalization, and faster and low-cost diagnoses.
elements.sent-2411-074: As example, predicting electrolyte imbalances (such as potassium and calcium) from ECG data can enable early detection of arrhythmias, allowing timely interventions in emergency or ICU settings.
elements.sent-2411-075: Additionally, predicting NTproBNP levels can aid in the early diagnosis and monitoring of heart failure, especially in remote monitoring or routine check-ups, enabling preventive action.
elements.sent-2411-076: Creatinine predictions from ECG could provide early insights into renal function, aiding in the detection of kidney dysfunction in outpatient or home care settings, preventing further damage through earlier referrals.
elements.sent-2411-077: First, our task definition relies on median threshold values inferred from MIMIC-IV and therefore do not cover dependencies of reference values on patient characteristics such as demographic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-078: This question will have to be revisited more thoroughly in future studies.
elements.sent-2411-079: For certain scenarios, also the differentiation into anomalously low and anomalously high values might be not finegrained enough and might necessitate to frame the prediction problem as a more finegrained ordinal classification or even as a regression problem.
elements.sent-2411-080: Second, the current setup of the monitoring task does not provide any insights into the time frame where the abnormality occurs.
elements.sent-2411-081: This could be mitigated by predicting future laboratory values at a particular future horizon up some tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-082: Here, we mainly use it to demonstrate the feasibility of providing prognostic insights into laboratory values.
elements.sent-2411-083: Finally, the approach should be tested on diverse cohorts to ensure it generalizes effectively before being deployed in clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-084: Second, we envision the use of explainable AI techniques, such as those put forward in \cite{wagner2023explaining or concept-based causal approaches \cite{alcaraz2024causalconceptts , could enhance our understanding on the mechanisms of how lab values impact the ECG.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: JMLA was responsible for data curation, formal analysis, and visualization, as well as writing the original draft.
elements.sent-2411-086: JMLA, and NS conducted the investigation and developed the methodology.
elements.sent-2411-087: NS oversaw the supervision of the project.
elements.sent-2411-088: Validation was carried out by NS.
elements.sent-2411-089: Finally, JMLA, and NS were involved in the review and editing of the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-090: The environment included Python 3.10.8, PyTorch 1.13.0, and PyTorch Lightning 1.8.0 to perform the computational tasks.
